content: A pure resistive circuit element $X$, when connected to an AC supply of peak voltage 200 V , gives a peak current of 5 A . A second circuit element $Y$, when connected to the same AC supply also gives the same value of peak current but the current lags behind by $90^{\circ}$. If the series combination of $X$ and $Y$ is connected to the same supply, what will be the rms value of current?
explanation:
Clearly first element is resistance and second element is inductor

$$ \begin{aligned} R & =X_L=\frac{200}{5}=40 \Omega \\ \therefore Z & =\sqrt{R_f^2+X_L^2}=40 \sqrt{2} \Omega \\ I_{\mathrm{rms}} & =\frac{V_{\mathrm{rms}}}{Z}=\frac{200 / \sqrt{2}}{40 \sqrt{2}}=\frac{5}{2} \mathrm{~A} \end{aligned} $$

content: A resistance $$R$$ and inductance $$L$$ and a capacitor $$C$$ all are connected in series with an $$\mathrm{AC}$$ supply. The resistance of $$R$$ is $$24 \mathrm{~ohm}$$ and for a given frequency, the inductive reactance of $$L$$ is 36 ohm and capacitive reactance of $$C$$ is $$24 \mathrm{~ohm}$$. If the current in the circuit is $$5 \mathrm{~amp}$$. Find the potential difference across $$R, L$$ and $$C$$.
explanation:
To determine the potential differences across the resistor $$R$$, inductor $$L$$, and capacitor $$C$$ in a series AC circuit, we can use Ohm's Law and the given impedances.

Given:

  • $$R = 24 \textrm{ ohm}$$

  • Inductive reactance $$X_L = 36 \textrm{ ohm}$$

  • Capacitive reactance $$X_C = 24 \textrm{ ohm}$$

  • Current in the circuit $$I = 5 \textrm{ amp}$$

First, we find the potential difference across each component using Ohm's Law:

  1. Potential difference across $$R$$:

$$ V_R = I \cdot R $$

$$ V_R = 5 \textrm{ amp} \cdot 24 \textrm{ ohm} $$

$$ V_R = 120 \textrm{ V} $$

  1. Potential difference across $$L$$:

$$ V_L = I \cdot X_L $$

$$ V_L = 5 \textrm{ amp} \cdot 36 \textrm{ ohm} $$

$$ V_L = 180 \textrm{ V} $$

  1. Potential difference across $$C$$:

$$ V_C = I \cdot X_C $$

$$ V_C = 5 \textrm{ amp} \cdot 24 \textrm{ ohm} $$

$$ V_C = 120 \textrm{ V} $$

Summarizing, the potential differences are:

  • $$V_R = 120 \textrm{ V}$$

  • $$V_L = 180 \textrm{ V}$$

  • $$V_C = 120 \textrm{ V}$$

Therefore, the correct answer is:

Option D: $$120, 180, 120 \textrm{ V}$$

content: A direct current of $$10 \mathrm{~A}$$ is superimposed on an alternating current $$i=10 \sin \omega t$$ flowing through the wire. The effective value of the resulting current will be.
explanation:
The resulting current is the sum of the direct current and the alternating current:

$$I = I_{dc} + I_{ac} = 10 + 10 \sin \omega t$$

To find the effective value (also known as the root-mean-square or RMS value), we need to calculate the square root of the mean of the squared current:

$$I_{rms} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{T} \int_{0}^{T} I^2 dt}$$

where T is the period of the alternating current.

Let's substitute the expression for I and simplify:

$$I_{rms} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{T} \int_{0}^{T} (10 + 10 \sin \omega t)^2 dt}$$

Expanding the square and integrating term by term:

$$I_{rms} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{T} \int_{0}^{T} (100 + 200 \sin \omega t + 100 \sin^2 \omega t) dt}$$

We know that the average value of $$ \sin \omega t $$ over a complete cycle is zero, and the average value of $$ \sin^2 \omega t $$ over a complete cycle is $$\frac{1}{2}$$. Therefore:

$$I_{rms} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{T} (100T + 0 + 100 \cdot \frac{1}{2} T)} = \sqrt{150}$$

Simplifying further:

$$I_{rms} = 5 \sqrt{6} \mathrm{~A}$$

Therefore, the correct answer is Option B: $$5 \sqrt{6} \mathrm{~A}$$.

content: A direct current of 6 A is superimposed on an alternating current I = 10 sin $$\omega$$t flowing through a wire. The effective value of the resulting current will be
explanation:
Given, $$I = 6 + 10\sin \omega t$$

$${I_{eft}} = \left[ {{{\int\limits_0^T {{I^2}dt} } \over {\int\limits_0^T {dt} }}} \right] = {\left[ {{1 \over T}\int\limits_0^T {{{(6 + 10\sin \omega t)}^2}dt} } \right]^{1/2}}$$

$$ = {\left[ {{1 \over T}\int\limits_0^T {(36 + 120\sin \omega t + 100{{\sin }^2}\omega t)dt} } \right]^{1/2}}$$

But as, $${1 \over T}\int\limits_0^T {\sin \omega t\,dt = 0} $$ and $${1 \over T}\int\limits_0^T {{{\sin }^2}\omega t = {1 \over 2}} $$

$$ \Rightarrow {I_{eff}} = {\left[ {36 + {1 \over 2} \times 100} \right]^{1/2}} = 9.27$$

Thus, $${I_{eff}} = 9.27\,A$$

content: In the circuit shown in the figure, the AC source gives a voltage V = 20 cos (2000t) neglecting source resistance, the voltmeter and ammeter reading will be
explanation:
Given, R1 = 8 $$\Omega$$

R2 = 2 $$\Omega$$

L = 5 mH

C = 50 $$\mu$$F

and V0 = 20 cos (2000t)

Impedance, $$Z = \sqrt {{R^2} + {{({X_L} - {X_C})}^2}} $$

As, here, $${X_L} = \omega L = 2000 \times 5 \times {10^{ - 3}} = 10\Omega $$

Similarly, $${X_L} = {1 \over {\omega C}} = {1 \over {2000 \times 50 \times {{10}^{ - 6}}}} = 10\Omega $$

$$\because$$ $${X_L} = {X_C}$$, Hence, $${i_{\max }} = {{{V_0}} \over Z} = {{20} \over {(8 + 2)}} = 2A$$

Hence, $${i_{rms}} = {{{i_{\max }}} \over {\sqrt 2 }} = {2 \over {\sqrt 2 }} = 1.41 \,A$$

and $$V = {R_2}\,{i_{rms}} = 1.41 \times 2 = 2.82\,V$$

content: The phasor diagram of a load represents which circuit?

options.content: R-L-C circuit with X<sub>L</sub> more than X<sub>C</sub>
R-L-C circuit with X<sub>L</sub> less than X<sub>C</sub>

explanation:
As shown in phasor diagram, voltage leads the current.

As in purely inductive circuit, current lags behind the voltage by an angle of 90$$^\circ$$, here angle is not 90$$^\circ$$ but it is lesser than that.

So, this type of case arise in R-L-C circuit when R-L-C load with inductive reactance is more than the capacitive reactance as shown below.

content: Three solenoid coils of same dimensions, same number of turns and same number of layers of windings are taken. Coil 1 has inductance L1 wounded by Mn wire of resistance 6 $$\Omega$$m$$-$$1, coil 2 with inductance L2 wounded by similar wire but in reverse direction in each layer. Coil 3 with inductance L3 wounded by a superconducting wire. The relation between their self inductances will be
explanation:
As coil 2 with inductance L2 has been wounded by a similar wire as coil 1 but the direction of windings is reversed in each layer, so flux through L2 will be zero.

As, L2 $$\propto$$ $$\phi$$ $$\Rightarrow$$ L2 = 0

Also, since the dimensions, number of turns and number of layers of windings are equal in coil 1 and 3.

So, L1 = L3

content: In the circuit shown in figure, the AC source has angular frequency $$\omega$$ = 2000 rad s$$-$$1. The amplitude of the current will be nearest to
explanation:
Given, $$\omega$$ = 2000 rad s$$-$$1

L = 5 mH = 5 $$\times$$ 10$$-$$3 H

C = 50 $$\mu$$F = 50 $$\times$$ 10$$-$$6 F

V = 30 V

Inductive reactance,

XL = $$\omega$$L = 2000 $$\times$$ 5 $$\times$$ 10$$-$$3 = 10 $$\Omega$$

and capacitive reactance,

$${X_C} = {1 \over {\omega C}} = {1 \over {50 \times {{10}^{ - 6}} \times 2000}} = {{100} \over {10}} = 10\Omega $$

$$\Rightarrow$$ XL = XC

The given circuit is resonant circuit.

Hence, impedance of circuit, Z = R = 4 + 6 + 0.5 = 10.5$$\Omega$$

Amplitude of current, $${I_0} = {V \over Z} = {{30} \over {10.5}} = 2.85$$ A
